reasoning:
1. June 1 (1 week): No FOMC meetings occur before this date, so the EFFR will rest firmly at ~4.33. The spread relies purely on a 7-day random walk in the 10-year Treasury yield. Given slight recent downward momentum in yields, I anchor near the FRED linear trend prediction of 0.48.

2. June 24 (1 month): This date falls after the June 17-18 FOMC meeting. Current market pricing (from CME FedWatch) suggests an approximate 35% chance of a 25bps rate cut. A cut directly lowers the EFFR, mechanically bumping the spread. This asymmetric upside risk pushes the expected probability above 0.50.

3. August 23 (3 months): This date accounts for the July FOMC meeting. Markets assign a >70% probability of at least one rate cut by late July. The expected value of the EFFR drops significantly here, which should widen the spread further. Since the yield curve is un-inverting and returning to a historic positive spread, the trend strongly favors an increase.

4. November 21 (6 months): Over a 6-month horizon, additional rate cuts are priced in, but variance in the 10-year yield expands enormously (susceptible to inflation surprises or debt/deficit shocks). Adhering to the instruction that uncertainty should increase over long horizons, the probability is manually adjusted back closer to 0.50 (maximum uncertainty) because the fundamental signal-to-noise ratio starts to decay.
